author: @AndreasEisele
text: That is truly fascinating; thank you so much for bringing the idea to our attention! I have an application in mind where the source of the power is a vertical-axis wind turbine, and on the other side there is a device that has a constant torque (e.g. lifting a bucket in a draw well). Obviously, the torque of the power source depends very much on the wind speed. I am wondering whether your contraption would work in such a setting. Do you think it could be worth trying?

author: @michaellowe3665
text: Current CVTs are like an expiration date on your car. They will wear out, and they will cost more than the car is worth to replace. I have never heard of them lasting as long as a typical 4 or 6 speed from 20 years ago. Maybe this is just how the car makers clear the path of high milage used cars so they can sell electric cars to people who dont want them. I also see the possibility of EVs using CVTs in reverse only for charging optimization. In order to get the maximum efficiency out of an EV motor during regen braking, it is best to keep the motor RPM in a narrow range. A CVT could be used to do this in conjunction with the brakes to balance the stopping feel and motor speed during deceleration. At least in this use case, they would be likely to last longer and they will definitely outlast the battery pack, which is the expiration date for an EV.

author: @mac-qt3wd
text:
Hi rob how's it going? Loving the stuff on gears, and as always I have a question if you'd be so kind please?
I've made a washing machine motor run direct from a solar panel and it's very impressive. (The speed/rpms).
I have also made a washing machine motor that is a generator and if you use a 6 volt battery to induce a current into the coils getting 70 volts dc using a battery drill to spin it (100 volts + if using a 12 volt battery, haven't tried 24 volts yet)
What I was wondering am I better off using the motor powered by a solar panel to rotate a shed load of magnets round lots of bigger copper coils or coupling the motor to the generator via a set of gears with a I dunno 10:1 ratio maybe?
If you go onto my channel you can see my latest uploads by their titles, otherwise if you reply on here I can then copy and paste my links in a reply to your reply without youtube disappearing them. Cheers rob

author: @charlieclelland5895
text:
Have you examined the transmission developed by George Constantinesco in the 1920s?. This was produced in France in the 1920s in a car and those who drove it said it was simple to drive and very flexible. The car was commercially unsuccessful but the Constantinesco transmission was used in Romanian railcars between the wars.
Constantinesco is best known for his hydraulically actuated interrupter gear in British WW1 fighter aircraft - this was said to be superior to any mechanical system.

author: @peterling1422
text: Following this series of videos I have wondered if you could use a CVT to form a or part of a suspension system. It would possibly be to heavy or complicated to be practical but that doesn't stop it from being possible (could vs should). Another aspect of suspension systems in cars/vehicles could also be things like toe, camber and other geometries that are often fixed not variable so I do wonder if gears or a CVT could be used to turn these properties to variable. Maybe eventually some good computing, cameras and time a reactive suspension system theoretically could optimise for all suspension geometries rather than just preload and damping (more common active suspension system variables). It doesn't need to be proved or delivered but fun to think about, best part of these videos to me is opening up to possiblities and activating the power of imagination/creativity to explore and we might just find good problems for these great solutions/ideas. Thanks Rob!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @derekfinch9586
text: I had a working steam engine as a kid and remember it had a governor that worked with centrifugal force. I had been thinking recently how such a mechanism could be employed to control the speed of a CVT. What an elegant solution! Thanks for sharing and the straightforward explanation.

replies.author: @Kineth1
replies.text: Belt-driven automatic CVTs that use a variable pulley diameter  are often used in scooters and small ATVs.  They use a "variator" plate with roller-shaped weights.  As the input speed increases or decreases, the rollers fly out or get pushed in, pushing on the variator and increasing or decreasing the effective radius of the pulley.  It is common to be able to buy lighter or heavier weights in order to tune the shifting behavior of this type of CVT.

author: @coachgeo
text: my 1970's Skid steer has manual controlled  belt style CVT.  Hydraulic power moves a pulley to one of two locations.

replies.author: @Kineth1
replies.text: My early 2000s riding mower also has a manual belt CVT.  One pulley is fixed diameter, the other spring loaded.  A lever on the console adjusts the idler tension and the increased tension forces the variable pulley to reduce diameter.

author: @l0I0I0I0
text:
Large motors have various wiring configs, switching and timing components. and capacitors used to ramp up to full speed in a few secomds. 
 It would be really cool and perhaps profitable if you could design this transition, low gear to high gear in a few seconds automtically and mechanically via gearing allowing minimum and optimized wiring configurations.  Such a design would allow for lower amperage requirement on startup and lower cost of larger diameter wiring and components, cheaper breakers, smaller or no motor capacitor, and fewer electronic components to make that transition.  Hope that makes since.😂

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: A car starter might also benifit.  Might want to add a disengage feature and or a breaking feature for advance designs.  A car would benefit by needing a smaller battery or perhaps a capacitor with this gearing. Disenging feature would be necessary.

replies.author: @joaomrtins
replies.text: All this is done eletriclly so you don't have to do it mechanically which would be much harder. Mechanical transmissions make much more sense when tramiting power between mechanical systems.

replies.author: @l0I0I0I0
replies.text: ​@joaomrtinsGood point.   Having worked on the electrical side of these systems, electronics seem to have a shorter life, where mechanical can be designed to hardly ever fail with occasional maintenance. Besides if its printed, once designed its just a repeat. But I know the industry has gone with electrical, but it would be awesome to see what he comes up with!
